teaching_objective: Child can segment CVC words and spell the target short /a/ in CVC words accurately.
child_friendly_explanation: A CVC word has three sounds. Say it slowly, tap first sound, vowel sound, last sound, then write one letter for each sound.
rule_explanation: In CVC words, each spoken sound should be represented in order, with one short vowel letter in the middle.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell only the loudest sounds, swap the short vowel for a nearby vowel, or leave off the first or final consonant.
first_exposure_progression: Orally segment a simple CVC word; count three sounds; identify the middle short-vowel sound; map each sound to a letter; practise with sound boxes before writing.
guided_practice_progression: Use high-frequency one-syllable CVC words. Prompt: say it, tap it, write first-vowel-last, then read it back; fade the sound boxes once accurate.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use quick mixed CVC retrieval and ask the child to re-read each word to check that all three sounds are present in order. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to underline the vowel, check the first and last consonants, and correct missing or swapped sounds.
example_selection_guidance: Start with regular one-syllable CVC words with clear short vowels; avoid digraphs, blends, long vowels, schwa, and tricky high-frequency words.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid contrasting all short vowels at first. Once segmentation is secure, use minimal contrasts where only the target vowel or consonant changes.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can treat sh as one /sh/ sound as one sound-spelling unit when reading and spelling.
child_friendly_explanation: Two letters can work as a team to make one sound. For this skill, look for sh as one /sh/ sound and write both letters in the right order.
rule_explanation: A consonant digraph uses two letters to represent one consonant sound; the child should treat the grapheme as one sound-spelling unit.
common_misconceptions: Child may write only one of the letters, reverse the letters, split the digraph into two sounds, or confuse it with a similar digraph.
first_exposure_progression: Introduce the digraph as two letters for one sound; say the sound; underline the digraph in a model word; segment words by sound units rather than by letters.
guided_practice_progression: Use simple support words with sh as one /sh/ sound. Prompt the child to circle the two-letter team, say the sound once, and spell the whole word; fade the circling prompt.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed digraph retrieval and proofreading. Ask the child to check whether the target sound has been written with the full two-letter team. Proofreading: In sentences, ask the child to spot words containing sh as one /sh/ sound, underline the digraph, and correct missing, reversed, or substituted letters.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common short words where sh as one /sh/ sound appears clearly at the stated final position; avoid words with multiple unfamiliar digraphs or complex morphology.
contrast_policy_guidance: Do not compare several digraphs at once during first exposure. Later, contrast one nearby sound-spelling pair at a time, such as sh/ch or voiced/unvoiced th.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can preserve every consonant sound in initial l-blends.
child_friendly_explanation: A blend is not one sound. You can hear each consonant, so each sound needs its own letter in the right order.
rule_explanation: In a blend, each consonant sound must be represented in the correct order; the child should spell both consonant sounds without omitting or reversing them.
common_misconceptions: Child may drop one consonant from the blend, reverse the order, or spell only the easier consonant they hear most strongly.
first_exposure_progression: Stretch the word orally; isolate each consonant in the blend; tap the sounds in order; write the letters while saying each sound.
guided_practice_progression: Use short common words with initial l-blends. Prompt the child to count the consonants in the blend, underline them after writing, and read the word back.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed blend retrieval and ask the child to check whether every consonant sound they can hear is represented in order. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to circle blends and check for missing or swapped consonants, especially at word beginnings and endings.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common one-syllable words with a single clear blend; avoid words that also contain new vowel spellings, silent letters, or difficult endings.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast with digraphs only after blends are secure: blends keep both sounds, digraphs make one sound.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can preserve every consonant sound in three-letter initial blends.
child_friendly_explanation: A blend is not one sound. You can hear each consonant, so each sound needs its own letter in the right order.
rule_explanation: In three-letter blends, all three consonant sounds must be represented in the correct order.
common_misconceptions: Child may drop one consonant from the blend, reverse the order, or spell only the easier consonant they hear most strongly.
first_exposure_progression: Stretch the word orally; isolate each consonant in the blend; tap the sounds in order; write the letters while saying each sound.
guided_practice_progression: Use short common words with three-letter initial blends. Prompt the child to count the consonants in the blend, underline them after writing, and read the word back.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed blend retrieval and ask the child to check whether every consonant sound they can hear is represented in order. contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast with digraphs only after blends are secure: blends keep both sounds, digraphs make one sound.

teaching_objective: Child can spell words that use the target long-vowel pattern: long /ai/ spelled ay at the end of words.
child_friendly_explanation: The same long vowel sound can be spelled in different ways. This skill teaches one spelling choice: long /ai/ spelled ay at the end of words.
rule_explanation: The long /ai/ sound can be spelled in different ways; children should learn that ay is commonly used at the end of words or syllables.
common_misconceptions: Child may choose a different long-vowel spelling that sounds right, forget a final silent e, or ignore position clues such as middle, final, or open syllable.
first_exposure_progression: Say the long-vowel sound; show the target spelling pattern; point out its usual position; underline the pattern; practise deciding whether the word matches the cue.
guided_practice_progression: Use support words with only this long-vowel spelling. Prompt the child to ask where the sound is, choose the spelling, then read the word back to confirm the long sound.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed long-vowel retrieval and ask the child to justify why long /ai/ spelled ay at the end of words is the spelling choice, not just whether the word sounds right. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to find long-vowel sounds, check the word-position cue, and correct overused alternative spellings or missing silent e.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common one-syllable or simple two-syllable words that clearly show the target long-vowel pattern; avoid rare or morphologically complex words.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid contrasting all spellings of the same long vowel at first. Once this pattern is secure, contrast with one related spelling using a clear cue such as final position, middle position, or split digraph.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

rule_explanation: Some words use split digraph e-e to represent the long /ee/ sound.
common_misconceptions: Child may choose a different long-vowel spelling that sounds right, forget a final silent e, or ignore position clues such as middle, final, or open syllable.
first_exposure_progression: Say the long-vowel sound; show the target spelling pattern; point out its usual position; underline the pattern; practise deciding whether the word matches the cue.
guided_practice_progression: Use support words with only this long-vowel spelling. Prompt the child to ask where the sound is, choose the spelling, then read the word back to confirm the long sound.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed long-vowel retrieval and ask the child to justify why long /ee/ spelled with the split digraph e_e is the spelling choice, not just whether the word sounds right. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to find long-vowel sounds, check the word-position cue, and correct overused alternative spellings or missing silent e.

teaching_objective: Child can spell /ar/ spelled ar and notice how r changes the vowel sound.
child_friendly_explanation: When a vowel is followed by r, the r can change the vowel sound. Look for the vowel + r spelling team.
rule_explanation: The /ar/ sound is commonly spelled with ar; children should learn this as a core r-controlled vowel pattern.
common_misconceptions: Child may use a plain short-vowel spelling, confuse ar/or/er spellings, or choose a spelling that matches accent rather than the written pattern.
first_exposure_progression: Say the r-controlled sound; underline the vowel + r pattern; compare it with a plain short-vowel word only briefly; practise writing the full r-controlled chunk.
guided_practice_progression: Use common words with /ar/ spelled ar. Prompt the child to find the r, mark the vowel + r chunk, and spell the whole word from chunks rather than isolated sounds.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed r-controlled retrieval and ask the child to circle the vowel + r chunk before checking the whole word. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan for r-controlled sounds, underline the r-controlled spelling, and correct plausible ar/or/er substitutions.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common one-syllable words with a clear r-controlled chunk; avoid rare alternative spellings and unstressed endings until the core pattern is secure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid comparing all r-controlled spellings at once. Later, contrast one pair at a time, especially ar/or/er, with clear oral and visual cues.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

common_misconceptions: Child may use a plain short-vowel spelling, confuse ar/or/er spellings, or choose a spelling that matches accent rather than the written pattern.
first_exposure_progression: Say the r-controlled sound; underline the vowel + r pattern; compare it with a plain short-vowel word only briefly; practise writing the full r-controlled chunk.
guided_practice_progression: Use common words with /or/ spelled oar or our. Prompt the child to find the r, mark the vowel + r chunk, and spell the whole word from chunks rather than isolated sounds.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed r-controlled retrieval and ask the child to circle the vowel + r chunk before checking the whole word. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan for r-controlled sounds, underline the r-controlled spelling, and correct plausible ar/or/er substitutions.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common one-syllable words with a clear r-controlled chunk; avoid rare alternative spellings and unstressed endings until the core pattern is secure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid comparing all r-controlled spellings at once. Later, contrast one pair at a time, especially ar/or/er, with clear oral and visual cues.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can use the relevant cue to choose /k/ spelled c before a, o or u.
child_friendly_explanation: One sound can have more than one spelling. This skill teaches when to use /k/ spelled c before a, o or u, using clues like position, next letter, word ending, or word family.
rule_explanation: The /k/ sound is usually spelled c before a, o, or u.
common_misconceptions: Child may use a spelling that sounds plausible but ignores the cue, overgeneralise a favourite spelling, or apply the rule to exception words.
first_exposure_progression: Introduce the sound; show the target spelling choice; explain the cue that makes this choice useful; classify a few words by the cue before spelling them.
guided_practice_progression: Use clear support words for this one choice only. Prompt: where is the sound, what comes next, what is the ending, and which spelling fits that clue?
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed retrieval and ask the child to justify the spelling choice for /k/ spelled c before a, o or u, not only to spell the word from memory. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to find the target sound, check the cue, and correct plausible but wrong spellings that ignore the cue.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common words where the spelling-choice cue is obvious; avoid exception words and words with several competing spelling decisions.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast is useful only after the target cue is known. Compare with one plausible alternative at a time, and make the cue explicit.

Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to find the target sound, check the cue, and correct plausible but wrong spellings that ignore the cue.
example_selection_guidance: Use a small, common, curriculum-relevant word set; avoid rare etymological words and avoid treating the pattern as fully predictable.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast is useful only after the target cue is known. Compare with one plausible alternative at a time, and make the cue explicit.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can apply the final consonant pattern for final /k/ spelled ck after a short vowel.
child_friendly_explanation: The end of a short word can need a special spelling pattern. Listen to the vowel before the final sound and check the ending.
rule_explanation: After a short vowel in a short word, final /k/ is usually spelled ck.
common_misconceptions: Child may write a single final consonant, choose a phonetically plausible ending, or miss the short-vowel cue before ck, tch or dge.
first_exposure_progression: Explain the short-vowel or final-letter cue; model how the ending is chosen; mark the vowel before the final sound; practise applying the pattern.
guided_practice_progression: Use short, regular words with clear short vowels. Prompt the child to identify the vowel sound before choosing the final spelling; then fade the prompt.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed final-ending retrieval and ask the child to check the vowel before the final consonant sound. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to inspect word endings for final /k/ spelled ck after a short vowel and correct single-letter or wrong-ending near-misses.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common one-syllable words that have a clear short vowel before the ending; avoid long-vowel words and longer words until the cue is secure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Do not contrast ck/tch/dge all at once. Later, contrast with long-vowel endings or single-letter endings so the short-vowel cue stays clear.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can recognise and spell split digraph a_e for long /ai/, including the final e.
child_friendly_explanation: The final e can work with an earlier vowel to make a long vowel sound. The two vowel letters are split by a consonant.
rule_explanation: In split digraph words, the final e works with the earlier vowel to show a long vowel sound.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell the long vowel with only the first vowel, forget the final e, or place the e next to the vowel instead of after the consonant.
first_exposure_progression: Show how the final e changes the vowel sound; mark the vowel, middle consonant, and final e; compare orally with a short-vowel word only after the pattern is shown.
guided_practice_progression: Use simple words with split digraph a_e for long /ai/. Prompt the child to find the first vowel and final e, draw the split connection, spell the word, and read it back.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed split-digraph retrieval and ask the child to check whether a long vowel needs a final e. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan for long-vowel words with a missing or misplaced final e and correct them.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common one-syllable split-digraph words with one middle consonant; avoid rare words, suffixes, and words with multiple vowel spellings initially.
contrast_policy_guidance: Use contrast with short-vowel CVC words only after the child understands the split digraph; keep contrasts minimal and visually clear.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can notice and spell FLOSS doubling after a short vowel.
child_friendly_explanation: Sometimes English doubles a consonant to show something important about the word, often that the vowel before it is short.
rule_explanation: In many short words, final /f/, /l/, /s/, and /z/ are doubled after a short vowel.
common_misconceptions: Child may write only one consonant, double a consonant when it is not needed, or miss the short-vowel cue.
first_exposure_progression: Introduce the short-vowel cue; show the doubled letter as part of the spelling pattern; have the child mark the vowel and doubled consonant before spelling.
guided_practice_progression: Use common words with FLOSS doubling after a short vowel. Prompt the child to check the vowel before the doubled consonant and to explain why the double letter is there.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed doubling retrieval and ask the child to check whether a double consonant is needed or whether one has been overused. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to look for short-vowel words and inspect the following consonant for missing or extra doubling.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common short words or familiar longer words with clear short vowels; avoid suffix-doubling rules unless this row is being deliberately connected later.
contrast_policy_guidance: Do not contrast all doubling reasons at first. Later, contrast base-word doubling with suffix-doubling so the child does not treat all double letters as one rule.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can spell words with silent k in initial kn even when not all letters are pronounced.
child_friendly_explanation: Some words have a letter you write but do not clearly hear. The hidden letter is part of the word’s spelling pattern.
rule_explanation: In some words, initial kn is spelled with k even though the k is not pronounced.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell only the sounds they hear and omit the silent letter, or may add a silent letter to words where it does not belong.
first_exposure_progression: Show the written pattern; say the word normally; mark the silent letter; practise copying and then spelling while naming the hidden letter.
guided_practice_progression: Use a small set of common words with silent k in initial kn. Prompt the child to spot the silent letter before covering the word and spelling it.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use spaced retrieval with quick visual checks, because silent-letter words often need memory as well as pattern knowledge. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan for words from the silent-letter family and check that the hidden letter has not been omitted.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common words from one silent-letter pattern only; avoid mixing several silent-letter families in first exposure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid broad contrast early. Later, contrast with similar pronounced clusters only when the child is not likely to insert silent letters everywhere.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use spaced retrieval with quick visual checks, because silent-letter words often need memory as well as pattern knowledge. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan for words from the silent-letter family and check that the hidden letter has not been omitted.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common words from one silent-letter pattern only; avoid mixing several silent-letter families in first exposure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid broad contrast early. Later, contrast with similar pronounced clusters only when the child is not likely to insert silent letters everywhere.

teaching_objective: Child can spell words with silent w after s even when not all letters are pronounced.
child_friendly_explanation: Some words have a letter you write but do not clearly hear. The hidden letter is part of the word’s spelling pattern.
rule_explanation: In some words, w is written after s but is not pronounced.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell only the sounds they hear and omit the silent letter, or may add a silent letter to words where it does not belong.
first_exposure_progression: Show the written pattern; say the word normally; mark the silent letter; practise copying and then spelling while naming the hidden letter.
guided_practice_progression: Use a small set of common words with silent w after s. Prompt the child to spot the silent letter before covering the word and spelling it.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use spaced retrieval with quick visual checks, because silent-letter words often need memory as well as pattern knowledge. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan for words from the silent-letter family and check that the hidden letter has not been omitted.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common words from one silent-letter pattern only; avoid mixing several silent-letter families in first exposure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid broad contrast early. Later, contrast with similar pronounced clusters only when the child is not likely to insert silent letters everywhere.

teaching_objective: Child can spell wa where a sounds like /o/ or /ɒ/ despite the vowel sound being influenced by w.
child_friendly_explanation: After w, the vowel letter may not sound the way you expect. Look at the written chunk, not just the vowel sound.
rule_explanation: After w, the letter a often sounds like /o/ or /ɒ/ rather than short /a/.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell the word by the vowel sound alone, writing o/er-type spellings and missing the w-controlled written pattern.
first_exposure_progression: Show how w changes the sound; underline the written chunk; say the word and then spell the chunk slowly; practise with a small family of words.
guided_practice_progression: Use common words with wa where a sounds like /o/ or /ɒ/. Prompt the child to find w first, then choose the written chunk that belongs with it.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed retrieval and ask the child to check words after w for the correct written vowel pattern. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan w-words and correct spellings that follow sound only rather than the taught written chunk.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common w-controlled words; avoid rare words and avoid mixing wa, war and wor until each chunk is separately secure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast wa, war and wor only after the child has met each pattern; keep the oral cue and written chunk visible.

teaching_objective: Child can spell negative contractions with not and explain what the apostrophe replaces.
child_friendly_explanation: A contraction squeezes two words together. The apostrophe shows where letters have been left out.
rule_explanation: In negative contractions, the apostrophe shows omitted letters from not.
common_misconceptions: Child may omit the apostrophe, put it in the wrong place, spell by sound only, or confuse a contraction with a possessive word.
first_exposure_progression: Show the two full words; cross out the omitted letters; replace them with an apostrophe; read the contraction; then spell it from the full form.
guided_practice_progression: Use common contractions of this type. Prompt the child to expand the contraction, identify the missing letters, place the apostrophe, and spell the joined form.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use sentence-level retrieval so the child chooses the contraction from meaning and grammar, not just from sound. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to expand each contraction to its full form and check that the apostrophe marks the missing letters.
example_selection_guidance: Start with high-frequency contractions that have transparent full forms; keep irregular contractions in a separate small set.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid contrasting contractions with possessives until apostrophe-for-missing-letters is secure. Later, use one contrast at a time.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can preserve the correct written vowel in words with weak initial a.
child_friendly_explanation: Some vowels sound weak or blurry when we say a word. Spelling has to keep the correct vowel even when your ear cannot hear it clearly.
rule_explanation: Unstressed vowels can sound unclear; children should preserve the correct written vowel even when it is weak in speech.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell the weak vowel with the sound they hear, omit it, or swap it for another common schwa spelling.
first_exposure_progression: Explain that unstressed vowels can sound unclear; clap or mark syllables; identify the weak vowel position; use a related word or known spelling chunk where helpful.
guided_practice_progression: Use common words with weak initial a. Prompt the child to say the word normally, then slowly, mark the weak vowel, and spell from the written pattern rather than sound alone.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use spaced retrieval and proofreading in longer words, because weak vowels often disappear during fast spelling. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to re-check each syllable, especially weak vowels at the start, middle, or end of the word.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common words where the weak-vowel position is clear and the rest of the word is familiar; avoid rare academic words at first.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid contrasting many schwa spellings early. Later, contrast words with the same weak sound but different written vowels using meaning, word family, or suffix cues.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can spell simple high-frequency tricky words by remembering the tricky part and any regular parts.
child_friendly_explanation: Some common words do not follow the easiest sound-spelling rule. Find the normal parts, then learn the tricky part carefully.
rule_explanation: Some short high-frequency words have unusual spellings and need to be remembered as fixed word forms.
common_misconceptions: Child may try to spell the whole word by sound, remember only the first or last letter, or over-apply a normal phonics rule.
first_exposure_progression: Read the word; map the regular sound-spelling parts; mark the tricky part; use say-cover-write-check or a mnemonic only for the part that needs memory.
guided_practice_progression: Use very small sets of high-frequency words. Prompt the child to name the tricky part before writing, then immediately check against the model.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use frequent short retrieval, mixed reading/writing, and sentence use so the word becomes automatic. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to pause on high-frequency tricky words and check the memorised tricky part, not just whether the word sounds right.
example_selection_guidance: Use high-frequency words the child actually needs in writing; avoid long lists and avoid mixing too many unrelated tricky words in one lesson.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid unnecessary contrast early. Later, contrast only with commonly confused high-frequency words or a tiny word family.

teaching_objective: Child can spell function words with fixed spellings by remembering the tricky part and any regular parts.
child_friendly_explanation: Some common words do not follow the easiest sound-spelling rule. Find the normal parts, then learn the tricky part carefully.
rule_explanation: Some very common grammar words are reduced in speech or have fixed spellings that cannot be reliably predicted from sound alone.
common_misconceptions: Child may try to spell the whole word by sound, remember only the first or last letter, or over-apply a normal phonics rule.
first_exposure_progression: Read the word; map the regular sound-spelling parts; mark the tricky part; use say-cover-write-check or a mnemonic only for the part that needs memory.
guided_practice_progression: Use very small sets of high-frequency words. Prompt the child to name the tricky part before writing, then immediately check against the model.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use frequent short retrieval, mixed reading/writing, and sentence use so the word becomes automatic. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to pause on high-frequency tricky words and check the memorised tricky part, not just whether the word sounds right.
example_selection_guidance: Use high-frequency words the child actually needs in writing; avoid long lists and avoid mixing too many unrelated tricky words in one lesson.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid unnecessary contrast early. Later, contrast only with commonly confused high-frequency words or a tiny word family.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can spell could, would and should by remembering the tricky part and any regular parts.
child_friendly_explanation: Some common words do not follow the easiest sound-spelling rule. Find the normal parts, then learn the tricky part carefully.
rule_explanation: The common words could, would, and should are spelled with ould. The mnemonic “Oh U Lucky Duck” can help children remember the unusual ould letter string.
common_misconceptions: Child may try to spell the whole word by sound, remember only the first or last letter, or over-apply a normal phonics rule.
first_exposure_progression: Introduce could, would and should as a tiny fixed family; mark ould as the tricky spelling; use the mnemonic only to support recall, not as a replacement for reading the word.
guided_practice_progression: Practise one ould word at a time in short sentences, prompting the child to say the word, name the ould chunk, then write and check it.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use frequent short retrieval, mixed reading/writing, and sentence use so the word becomes automatic. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to pause on high-frequency tricky words and check the memorised tricky part, not just whether the word sounds right.
example_selection_guidance: Use high-frequency words the child actually needs in writing; avoid long lists and avoid mixing too many unrelated tricky words in one lesson.
contrast_policy_guidance: Do not mix with ordinary /ould/ or /old/ words at first. Later, contrast could/would/should only as a small fixed family.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can split words using open and closed syllable division to support spelling.
child_friendly_explanation: Longer words are easier when you break them into useful chunks. Each chunk helps you decide what letters to write.
rule_explanation: Children can split longer words into syllables to help decide whether vowels are short or long.
common_misconceptions: Child may try to spell the whole word from memory, lose a syllable, or choose a vowel spelling without checking whether the syllable is open or closed.
first_exposure_progression: Say the word; clap the syllables; draw the split; mark the vowel in each syllable; spell one syllable at a time before joining the whole word.
guided_practice_progression: Use common two-syllable words with clear open and closed syllable division. Prompt the child to split first, spell each chunk, then blend the chunks back into the full word.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed longer-word retrieval and ask the child to mark syllable breaks before spelling difficult parts. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to check that no syllable has been skipped and that each vowel spelling matches the syllable type or chunk.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common two-syllable words with clear splits; avoid words with schwa, unusual roots, or multiple suffixes until later.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast open and closed syllables only after the child can split words reliably; keep the contrast to one vowel decision at a time.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

teaching_objective: Child can recognise and spell the final consonant + le syllable -ble ending as a stable final syllable.
child_friendly_explanation: Some words end with a final syllable that sounds like /ul/. You still need to write the full consonant + le pattern.
rule_explanation: Many words end with a final consonant + le syllable; children should preserve the full final syllable.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell the final syllable as ul, el or l, omit the e, or choose the wrong consonant before le.
first_exposure_progression: Say the word; clap the final syllable; show the consonant + le ending; underline the ending as one stable chunk; practise spelling the whole final syllable.
guided_practice_progression: Use common words with final consonant + le syllable -ble. Prompt the child to find the final /ul/ sound, choose the correct consonant + le chunk, and read the whole word back.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed final-syllable retrieval and ask the child to check the final /ul/ syllable visually. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to inspect final /ul/ words for missing e, wrong vowel, or wrong consonant before le.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common words where the final consonant + le chunk is clear; avoid words with difficult earlier syllables initially.
contrast_policy_guidance: Do not contrast every consonant + le ending at once. Later, compare one pair, such as -ble and -ple, only if confusion appears.

teaching_objective: Child can recognise and spell stable final syllable -tion as a stable ending in longer words.
child_friendly_explanation: Some longer words have a final chunk that appears again and again. Learn the chunk as one spelling unit.
rule_explanation: Many longer words end with the stable final syllable -tion, often sounding like /shun/.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell the ending by sound only, choose another /shun/ or /chuh/ spelling, or omit a vowel in the final syllable.
first_exposure_progression: Introduce the final stable syllable; say it as a chunk; underline the ending in a model word; then build words by base or stem plus the stable ending.
guided_practice_progression: Use common longer words with stable final syllable -tion. Prompt the child to identify the final chunk before spelling the rest of the word.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed longer-word retrieval and ask the child to justify the final stable syllable spelling. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to scan word endings, underline the stable syllable, and correct sound-only endings.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common curriculum words where the final syllable is clear and the stem is not too difficult; avoid rare academic words until secure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Do not contrast tion/sion/cian/cial/tial too early. Later, contrast one ending pair at a time with meaning or spelling cues.

teaching_objective: Child can preserve all syllables when spelling reduced middle syllables.
child_friendly_explanation: In long words, some syllables are easy to miss because we say them quickly. Slow the word down and make sure every chunk is written.
rule_explanation: Longer words may contain middle syllables that are weak in speech; children should preserve the full syllable in spelling.
common_misconceptions: Child may omit a weak syllable, collapse two syllables into one, or spell only the stressed part of the word.
first_exposure_progression: Say the word naturally; say it slowly; clap or box each syllable; write each syllable or meaningful chunk before joining the full word.
guided_practice_progression: Use familiar longer words with reduced middle syllables. Prompt the child to count syllables, mark the weak part, and check that every syllable has letters.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use spaced retrieval in sentences and ask the child to slow down longer words before checking them. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to re-read long words syllable by syllable and correct missing weak chunks.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common longer words the child uses in writing; avoid rare curriculum words until syllable-counting is fluent.
contrast_policy_guidance: Avoid contrast early. Later, contrast the fast spoken form with the careful spelling form to make the missing syllable visible.

teaching_objective: Child can use meaning parts to spell finding the base word.
child_friendly_explanation: Big words are easier when you find the smaller meaning parts: base words, prefixes, suffixes, compounds, roots, or related words.
rule_explanation: Longer words often contain a base word; finding the base helps children preserve the spelling.
common_misconceptions: Child may spell the whole word by sound, lose the base word, change an affix unnecessarily, or miss a stable root/meaning chunk.
first_exposure_progression: Introduce the meaning part; identify it in a clear model word; mark the boundary; explain what the part means or does; then spell from parts.
guided_practice_progression: Use words where the target base, affix, root, compound part, or related-word cue for finding the base word is clear. Prompt the child to find the meaning part first, then spell and join the parts.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed morphology retrieval and ask the child to explain the base, prefix, suffix, compound, root, or related word that supports the spelling. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to box the meaning parts and check that the base/root spelling has been preserved where the rule says it should.
example_selection_guidance: Start with familiar words where the meaning part is visible; avoid opaque word histories and multiple new morphemes in one item.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast related morphemes only after the target part is secure. Avoid teaching several prefixes, suffixes, or roots together unless the comparison is the lesson target.

guided_practice_progression: Use familiar base words for drop-e before -ing. Prompt the child to say the base, choose the ending, apply the rule, and read the new word in a sentence.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed inflection retrieval and ask the child to explain the base word and ending before checking spelling. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to underline the base, circle the ending, and check for missing endings or incorrect spelling changes.
example_selection_guidance: Start with familiar silent-e bases; avoid words where e is retained for soft c/g or other advanced reasons.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast rule conditions only after the base action is secure. Compare one condition at a time, such as consonant + y versus vowel + y.

teaching_objective: Child can remember and use irregular past tense verbs as fixed irregular forms.
child_friendly_explanation: Some changed word forms do not follow the normal ending rule. You have to learn the whole changed form.
rule_explanation: Some verbs do not form the past tense with -ed and must be learned as irregular forms.
common_misconceptions: Child may add the regular ending, mix regular and irregular forms, or choose a form that sounds plausible but is not standard.
first_exposure_progression: Show the base form and the irregular form together; explain the meaning change; use the irregular form in a sentence; practise retrieval from the base.
guided_practice_progression: Use a small set of high-frequency irregular forms for irregular past tense verbs. Prompt the child to say the base, say the changed form, and spell the whole changed word.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use frequent sentence-level retrieval so the child practises choosing the irregular form in context. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to check whether a regular ending has been wrongly added where an irregular form is needed.
example_selection_guidance: Choose high-frequency irregular forms that appear often in child writing; avoid long lists or rare literary forms.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast with the regular rule only after the irregular form has been learnt; otherwise the regular pattern may interfere.

teaching_objective: Child can choose the correct spelling for to, too and two using meaning and grammar.
child_friendly_explanation: These words can sound the same or very similar, so your ear is not enough. Use the sentence meaning to choose the right spelling.
rule_explanation: Some common function-word homophones sound the same but have different meanings; children must use the sentence meaning to choose the correct spelling.
common_misconceptions: Child may choose by sound alone, use the most familiar spelling everywhere, or ignore grammar clues such as possession, place, number, verb, or contraction.
first_exposure_progression: Teach to for direction/verb link, too for also or too much, and two for the number; use one sentence frame for each before mixing.
guided_practice_progression: Prompt the child to ask: number, also/too much, or ordinary to? Then choose the spelling.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed sentence proofreading and ask the child to justify the spelling choice by meaning, not pronunciation. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to stop at each homophone, replace it with its meaning or full form, and correct the spelling if the sentence meaning does not fit.
example_selection_guidance: Use clear sentences with familiar vocabulary and only one homophone decision at a time; avoid ambiguous, idiomatic, or joke sentences during first exposure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast is necessary, but introduce only two meanings at first unless the set itself has three forms. Add the full set after the child can explain each role.
source_use_note: Human-authored and human-reviewed Teaching Dictionary micro-skill content supplied by Katie Sanderson; approved for candidate readiness validation, not imported to Supabase by this pass.

contrast_policy_guidance: Introduce the three-way contrast slowly. If overloaded, teach there/their first, then add they’re with the expansion test.

teaching_objective: Child can choose the correct spelling for peace and piece using meaning and grammar.
child_friendly_explanation: These words can sound the same or very similar, so your ear is not enough. Use the sentence meaning to choose the right spelling.
rule_explanation: Peace and piece sound the same but have different meanings; children should use meaning to choose the correct spelling.
common_misconceptions: Child may choose by sound alone, use the most familiar spelling everywhere, or ignore grammar clues such as possession, place, number, verb, or contraction.
first_exposure_progression: Teach each word’s meaning or grammar job; put it in a clear sentence frame; for contractions, expand to the full form; then choose the spelling from meaning.
guided_practice_progression: Use short, unambiguous sentences for peace and piece. Prompt the child to say what the word means or does in the sentence before spelling it.
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed sentence proofreading and ask the child to justify the spelling choice by meaning, not pronunciation. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to stop at each homophone, replace it with its meaning or full form, and correct the spelling if the sentence meaning does not fit.
example_selection_guidance: Use clear sentences with familiar vocabulary and only one homophone decision at a time; avoid ambiguous, idiomatic, or joke sentences during first exposure.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast is necessary, but introduce only two meanings at first unless the set itself has three forms. Add the full set after the child can explain each role.

teaching_objective: Child can use the relevant cue to choose /ow/ spelled ou.
child_friendly_explanation: One sound can have more than one spelling. This skill teaches when to use /ow/ spelled ou, using clues like position, next letter, word ending, or word family.
rule_explanation: The /ow/ sound is often spelled ou in the middle of words.
common_misconceptions: Child may use a spelling that sounds plausible but ignores the cue, overgeneralise a favourite spelling, or apply the rule to exception words.
first_exposure_progression: Introduce the sound; show the target spelling choice; explain the cue that makes this choice useful; classify a few words by the cue before spelling them.
guided_practice_progression: Use clear support words for this one choice only. Prompt: where is the sound, what comes next, what is the ending, and which spelling fits that clue?
review_proofreading_progression: Review: Use mixed retrieval and ask the child to justify the spelling choice for /ow/ spelled ou, not only to spell the word from memory. Proofreading: In proofreading, ask the child to find the target sound, check the cue, and correct plausible but wrong spellings that ignore the cue.
example_selection_guidance: Start with common one-syllable words where the diphthong spelling is clear; separate medial and final position choices where possible.
contrast_policy_guidance: Contrast is useful only after the target cue is known. Compare with one plausible alternative at a time, and make the cue explicit.
